title: Synaptic weight over time, for identified synapses or a declared group

purpose.canonicalQuestion: How did the weight of a set of individually identified synapses — or the explicitly named aggregate of a declared group of synapses whose membership may change — evolve over a declared time window, under one declared observation semantics that says exactly what was observed and when?
cannotEstablish: What the weight was between two observations. An event-updated weight is drawn as a step because the value is HELD from its update until the next one; a polled or sampled weight is drawn as a line whose segments are a drawing convention. Neither asserts that anything was measured in between.

That a flat stretch means the plasticity rule was inactive. In NEST an STDP synapse is updated LAZILY, only at a presynaptic spike: postsynaptic activity in between accrues in the postsynaptic trace and is applied at the NEXT presynaptic spike.
That the drawn step is the weight a spike arriving at that instant would have carried. It is the STORED weight, and plasticity pending from postsynaptic activity has not been applied to it yet.

That a flat stretch means the rule stopped acting. A weight sitting at a hard bound (an STDP `Wmax`) stops moving while the rule keeps firing. Saturation and inactivity are visually identical, and this figure cannot separate them.

That the plasticity rule named in `synapseModel` CAUSED the change. Spike-timing-dependent plasticity, homeostatic scaling, structural rewiring and a caller's own `SetStatus` write all produce a weight trajectory, and a weight trajectory alone does not distinguish them.

The physical strength of the synapse. A `nest:weight` is simulator-defined: the same value 10.0 may act as a postsynaptic current amplitude under one neuron model and as a conductance under another. It has no SI mapping, is never converted, and is never compared across simulators.

That the plotted synapses are representative of a projection, a population, or a network. The aggregate is over EXACTLY the declared members, its denominator is the number of members that actually had a value at that time, and both counts are printed.

That the declared initial weight or the declared bounds are true. They are caller or model declarations. An initial weight may seed an explicitly attributed leading hold or derived aggregate, and either declaration may be shown as an optional reference line, but Cortexel does not verify the declaration against the simulator. Bounds never clamp, correct, or suppress an observed value: when that member series is displayed, a weight above a declared `Wmax` remains at its observed value; when member geometry is hidden by aggregate-only display, its exact observed row remains in the complete table.

That a poll captured every update. Polling `GetConnections(...).get('weight')` at declared times reveals only the stored value at those times: an update and a counter-update falling between two polls cancel and leave no trace at all.
Anything about synapses held on another MPI rank. A rank holds the connections whose TARGET it owns, so a rank-local weight recorder never saw the rest of them.

That two parallel synapses (a multapse) between the same pair are the same synapse. They are distinct edges with distinct ids and are never merged — and a source that cannot tell them apart cannot be used to plot either of them individually.

normalization: Parallel-array lengths are checked BEFORE any sort. A time vector and a weight vector of different lengths are never paired by best effort: a single off-by-one shifts every update onto the wrong event and moves a potentiation to a spike that did not cause it.

Recorder output is NOT assumed chronological. Samples are stably sorted by (time, original ordinal) WITHIN one edge series only. Sorting never crosses edges: the ordering that matters belongs to the synapse that owns it, and merging two synapses' updates into one sorted stream would destroy the identity this figure exists to preserve. Original ordinal lineage is retained on every carrier derived from caller array rows: source observations, caller reconstruction vertices, and source-state witnesses. Derived aggregate evaluations and declared initial-state carriers have no source-array ordinal and say so with null lineage.

Duplicate timestamps within one edge require an explicit policy. Revision 2 refuses every duplicate in an `event_updated` series: one request-wide before/after discriminator cannot identify the side or sequential event represented by each same-time row, and stable array order alone is not a scientific event-order claim.

A named duplicate `aggregate` is available only for point samples and linear reconstruction vertices, where repeated carriers at one time can be collapsed rather than interpreted as two sides of an update. Actual duplicate `keep_replicates` is refused for every weight observation kind: event rows lack side/order identity; repeated point samples would become an invented vertical trajectory; and two reconstruction y values at one x are not a function. Last-write-wins is never applied.

`null` in a weight array is a MISSING observation and it BREAKS THE HOLD. The interval from a null-valued update to the next observed update is undefined and is drawn as a gap. Holding the previous value across it would assert that the update did nothing, which is precisely the thing that was not observed.

Non-finite values are refused at parse and snapshot: a weight of NaN or Infinity is not a measurement. Negative zero is ACCEPTED and canonicalized to `0` (RFC 8785). -0.0 and 0.0 are the same weight; the distinction survives only in JavaScript, where preserving it would break digest reproducibility.

Every time value is converted into the unit declared on `window`, which is the single analysis frame of the figure. A conversion is one multiplication within one dimension; it never chains through an intermediate unit and never crosses a dimension. Every conversion is recorded in the ordered per-series preparation receipt with its source unit, target unit, and exact rational factor; the family emits one bounded batch operation rather than one artifact operation per series.

Weights are NEVER converted when their unit's dimension is `simulator_defined`. `nest:weight` has `toCanonical: null`: there is no factor, so there is no conversion, no SI mapping, and no pooling with any other code — including another simulator-defined code.

The analysis window is half-open [start, stop) by default. An update exactly at `stop` is excluded and counted. Under an explicitly closed stop, a value-after update at the terminal instant has zero in-window duration but remains an included source fact, so it receives a mandatory authority-bound terminal marker even when optional observation markers are off; otherwise the previous held value would visually erase the update.

Each edge declares a `recordedInterval`: the span over which the source was actually observing THIS synapse. It must have a positive-duration intersection with the analysis window after exact unit conversion. Its declared open/closed stop is honored for individual traces. Derived hold aggregates require `[start,stop)` recording intervals because a closed recording stop creates a left-sided availability transition that the present aggregate carrier cannot encode. A held step is drawn only inside the effective intersection and is never extended past it.
When `eventKinds` is present it is an exact parallel array with one cause label per observation time. A shorter list is refused rather than shifted or padded with an invented `unknown` cause.

Membership intervals are half-open [enter, exit) and carry the membership block's declared unit. There is no boundary parameter: a closed interval would make a synapse simultaneously a member and not a member at the instant another interval begins. Series edge ids, the aggregate group id, and membership edge ids are globally collision-free; every member resolves to exactly one series, and one member's intervals are strictly positive, ordered and non-overlapping. Cortexel never resolves membership with last-write-wins, silently unions overlaps, or treats a reversed span as empty.

Every observation, analysis-window endpoint, recorded-interval endpoint, membership endpoint and declared evaluation time that can affect inclusion is converted exactly once into the window unit. Correct rounding is monotone; Cortexel groups equal converted values and refuses the request unless every value in such a group is also the same exact physical time. This finite-set order-embedding rule prevents a rounded boundary collision from fabricating or erasing membership/recording decisions. Positive interval widths must also survive conversion within the registered eight-unit-roundoff effect tolerance.

The declared order of `series` is canonical. It fixes identity/provenance order plus legend and palette assignment. Exact BigInt mean and Type-7 quantile arithmetic is permutation-invariant and rounds only once; membership-array permutation therefore cannot change a numeric aggregate or replace series order as the canonical identity order. Revision 2 reports no extremizer identity or tie set for min/max and makes no such claim.
Edges are NEVER reordered by weight, by change magnitude, or by any other observed property. A sort by what the data did is a claim about the data.

The exact raw `NetworkScopeV1` is retained once in the canonical request, and the live in-process `requestDigest` covers that request. Authority rows and summary facts expose only the bounded canonical scope projection, so `mergedRanks` is not repeated with world-size cardinality. Artifact 1.0 table assurance is `shape_only` and cell bytes remain unbound; it provides no detached verification or persisted assurance receipt.
derivation: The observation semantics are declared ONCE, for the whole request, in `data.observation`: they are a property of the source recording, not of one synapse. One figure may not mix them. An aggregate that held a point sample, or that averaged a step-held value with a continuously varying one, would combine two incompatible assumptions about unobserved time.

event_updated + value_after_update (right-continuous): the sample at t IS the weight for [t, next observed t). This is the honest reading when the source writes the weight AFTER the update that the event triggered. Before the first observed update the value is undefined, unless `initialWeight` is declared, in which case the step begins at the start of the recorded interval with that value.

event_updated + value_before_update (left-continuous): the sample at t is the weight in force on (previous observed t, t] — it DESCRIBES THE INTERVAL ENDING AT t. Individual and caller-declared preaggregated traces may use this convention: the first sample closes the interval from recorded start to t, and the trace ends at the last supplied sample because the weight after that update is unknown. Cortexel-derived aggregates refuse it in revision 2 because their changing denominator and half-open terminal need side-qualified carriers the artifact does not have.

Cortexel never converts one update convention into the other. They differ by exactly one inter-update interval on every step edge, and a silent conversion would draw a potentiation as having happened before the spike that caused it.

point_sample: each sample is an instantaneous observation of a continuously varying weight; consecutive finite samples of one edge are joined by a straight segment only within one valid render run. Missing values and membership/recording availability-signature transitions break runs. Every surviving segment is a stated drawing convention and is disclosed; it asserts nothing about the values between the samples, and an update that occurred and was undone between two polls leaves no trace.

interpolated_trajectory: the caller/source-system's own reconstruction between supplied vertices. Revision 2 accepts only `linear`, binds the declared algorithm, interpolant and author in the table and accessible summary, and labels the vertices as reconstruction nodes rather than observations. Its vertices never enter the source-observation count and may not be members of a derived hold aggregate. Every vertex must already lie inside the effective analysis/recording window: silently discarding an outside knot can change geometry inside the window, so callers must pre-clip and declare the exact retained linear vertices.

Derived aggregate, hold evaluation (`hold_last_observed_at_union_times`, `hold_last_observed_at_declared_times`): legal only when `data.observation.kind` is `event_updated`, because a hold is what an event-updated weight actually does between updates and is a fabrication for anything else. The union grid contains every accepted update, membership boundary, effective recorded boundary, window start, and an inclusive window stop. A declared grid must contain that complete discontinuity closure; otherwise the aggregate would be drawn as holding across an omitted change.

Under the supported derived `value_after_update` hold, a member's value at t is its last non-null source update at or before t, restricted to t inside that member's recorded interval and membership lifetime. A source update below analysis start may supply carry-in state and is retained as an explicit source-state witness. Adjacent half-open membership intervals are one continuous lifetime, while a positive-duration gap resets held state: an observation or recorded-start initial value from the earlier lifetime never contributes after re-entry. A member with no defined value in its active lifetime at t does not contribute.

Derived aggregate, `shared_sample_grid`: legal only when `data.observation.kind` is `point_sample` and every member carries an IDENTICAL accepted time vector, compared elementwise after the order-preserving registered-unit conversion; the aggregate is formed per sample index. `keep_replicates` is refused when duplicates exist because no cross-synapse replicate identity says which repeated readings should be paired by index; a named within-synapse duplicate aggregate is required first. No interpolation is performed. A straight aggregate or member run breaks at every membership/recording availability-signature transition strictly between sample times—even when some member remains available—so it never draws continuously through an unobserved denominator or population change.

Aggregate denominators. memberCount(t) is the number of declared members whose membership intervals contain t. contributingCount(t) is the number of those that also have a defined, non-null value at t. THE DENOMINATOR OF `mean` IS contributingCount(t) — never memberCount(t), and never the number of declared edges.
Both counts are computed at every evaluation time and both reach the table and the artifact, so an aggregate taken over fewer synapses than the group contains says so on its own row.

mean is the correctly rounded exact binary64 sum divided by contributingCount. Median and every requested empirical quantile use Hyndman-Fan Type 7 with the supplied probability interpreted as its exact binary64 rational; index selection, interpolation and endpoint products remain BigInt rationals until one final ties-to-even rounding. Sample standard deviation uses the exact translated integer variance identity and one directly rounded rational square root. It is descriptive dispersion around a mean and is offered only with a mean center. Nonzero underflow and finite-range overflow are refused rather than reported as zero, NaN or infinity. min/max are exact extrema.

contributingCount(t) == 0 produces a MISSING aggregate (null) at t. It never produces 0.0. A drawn zero at a time when no synapse existed, or when no member had a recorded value, is a fabricated measurement — it reads as 'every synapse had zero weight' when the truth is 'nothing was observed'.

`sum` is not offered as an aggregate method. Over a membership that changes size, a sum conflates a change in the number of synapses with a change in their weights, and registry 1.0 has no disclosure that can carry that conflation. The member and contributing counts are in the table; a caller who wants a total can form it and declare it as a pre-aggregated series.

Every identified raw edge requires `uncertainty:none` in revision 2. One series is one synapse from one run, and the request has no aligned repeat universe, repeat-level values, central estimator, or bootstrap provenance that could justify an SD, SE, interval, or reconstructed uncertainty band at each time. This applies to individual, derived, point, event and interpolated raw modes alike. Caller-declared preaggregated input may carry across-member dispersion because its memberCounts, contributingCounts, basis, sampleCount, estimator identities and interval method are explicitly checked, while Cortexel discloses that omitted members prevent re-derivation.

The aggregate's own dispersion is computed by Cortexel from the contributing members under the declared `dispersion`, and is therefore a library-generated fact rather than a caller assertion. Sample standard deviation needs at least two contributors and is offered only around a mean; at one contributor its value and sampleCount are both missing. Standard error and confidence intervals are refused: the exact declared ensemble carries no sampling estimand, sampling design, exchangeability model, repeat universe, or coverage procedure. Quantile probabilities must be strictly ordered. Every selected derived member and every selected initial weight that can enter a hold uses one exact weight-unit code; rounding heterogeneous physical units independently before pooling is refused because it can erase variation or double-round the statistic.

Pre-aggregated input is re-checked, not trusted, as far as it can be: lengths, exact decision-boundary embedding, spacing fidelity, strictly increasing in-window evaluation times (including a closed stop), ordering of uncertainty bounds, integrality of the counts, contributingCount <= memberCount, and the exact row identity value is null if and only if contributingCount is zero. A non-none aggregate uncertainty must use the registered `ensemble_members` basis; distinct synapses are not silently called statistical replicates. Empirical quantile/range sampleCount equals contributingCount whenever at least one member contributes; sample standard deviation is allowed only around a mean, requires at least two, and is missing with a null count below that threshold. Standard error, confidence intervals, and credible intervals are refused because the request has no inferential design or verified posterior. An ensemble range must contain mean/median and equal a declared min/max endpoint; Type-7 quantile claims obey the monotone relations implied by a declared min, max or median, and every empirical endpoint equals the aggregate when one member contributes. The accessible summary names both the caller's aggregate method and intervalMethod, including `declared_by_source`, and reports only maximum concurrent membership because unique synapse cardinality was not supplied. Cortexel cannot re-derive the aggregate — it never saw the members — and discloses that.

Every Cortexel-derived hold aggregate in revision 2 requires `value_after_update`. A derived value-before series would need side-qualified denominator transitions and an authority-bound terminal carrier to paint a half-open window's trailing interval without moving a future state backward. Those carriers do not exist, so even a constant-membership counterexample is refused rather than rendered incompletely.

Every observed weight, uncertainty endpoint, initial weight and one-sided or two-sided bound must survive the renderer's one-time conversion into the chosen weight-axis unit as a finite nonzero value whenever the source value is nonzero; distinct supplied values must retain their spacing within the registered eight-unit-roundoff effect tolerance. When both bounds exist their exact lower/upper order and positive converted width are checked. Cortexel NEVER clamps, rounds, or corrects an observed weight against them: when its member series is displayed, a weight above a declared hard bound remains at the observed value; otherwise the exact hidden-member row and violation remain in the complete table.

Complexity: O(n log n) per edge for the stable sort, O(n) for window filtering and duplicate resolution, and O(c log c) for c decision-critical converted times in the finite-set collision check. For E aggregate evaluation times and M selected members, the revision-2 evaluator visits Θ(E×M) member states even though each member's interval and observation cursors advance only linearly; exact quantiles add Θ(sum_t k_t log k_t) for contributor counts k_t, while exact mean and dispersion arithmetic add Θ(sum_t k_t). The complete returned-row preflight saturates before geometry allocation when the 500-row contract limit cannot be met.
scopeRules: A series is ONE synapse from one run. It is never formed by concatenating two synapses, two seeds, or two runs; those are separate edges with separate ids, or an aggregate with a declared membership.

Multapses are never merged. Two synapses between the same (source, target) pair are two edges with two ids and two traces. A source that cannot attribute a weight event to one of them (NEST's weight recorder exposes no per-synapse identity) cannot be used to plot either of them individually.

A NetworkScopeV1 is mandatory. `snapshotTime` is NOT required here — the figure's time axis IS its temporal extent — and when it is supplied it names the moment at which the connection SET was enumerated, which is the moment the declared membership is true of.

`mpi_target_rank_local` is ACCEPTED and disclosed. Under NEST's MPI semantics a rank holds the connections whose TARGET it owns, so a rank-local weight recorder observed only those synapses. Every claim in the figure stays local: an aggregate under this scope is an aggregate over the declared, rank-local members and is never the group's mean.

`sampled` is ACCEPTED and disclosed, for the same reason and with the same limit: the aggregate's denominator is the DECLARED membership, so the number is well defined, but it is a statistic of the declared members and never of the projection they were sampled from.

A caller-supplied `groupLabel` is display text. The accessible summary always states the member count beside it, so a label such as 'all E to E synapses' can never do the work of a completeness claim that no scope in this contract supports.

In derived mode the membership edge-id set must equal the data.series edge-id set exactly. A foreign member is a denominator entry with no source behind it; an unused raw series would enter the complete table and carrier counts without being drawn or influencing the aggregate. Both mismatches are refused rather than silently dropped or mixed.

Every weight on the value axis must share one unit dimension, and the duplicate-free set of distinct synapse models must exactly match the explicit comparability declaration for physical and simulator-defined units alike. A derived pool is stricter: every selected value and contributing initial state uses one exact unit code so independently rounded conversions never enter the statistic. `nest:weight` is one code covering values whose physical meaning differs by synapse and neuron model.

`individual` display refuses more than 8 edges with RENDER_SERIES_LIMIT_EXCEEDED: the CVD-safe palette (Okabe-Ito) carries exactly 8 style tuples (`categoricalSeries.maxStableSeries`), so a ninth could only be drawn by minting a style outside it. Larger sets go through `aggregate_derived_with_members`, where members carry no colour or legend identity and are recoverable only from the table.

oneOf.description: Edges mode: the raw per-synapse observations. This is the honest input whenever the observations exist, because everything else in the figure — the aggregate, its denominators, the dispersion — is then DERIVED and checkable rather than asserted.

window.description: The analysis window and the single time frame of the figure. Every edge's times are converted into its unit. Half-open by default: an update exactly at `stop` is excluded and counted.

scope.description: Where these synapses were observed. Mandatory: a weight trace from a rank-local recorder and one from a merged global snapshot are different evidence, and only the scope can say which this is. `snapshotTime`, when supplied, names the moment the connection set was enumerated — the moment the declared membership is true of.

observation.description: What a sample IS, declared ONCE for the whole request because it is a property of the source recording. Mixing kinds in one figure is not offered: an aggregate that held a point sample, or averaged a step-held value with a continuously sampled one, would combine two incompatible assumptions about unobserved time.

oneOf.description: The source wrote a value at each synaptic update (a NEST weight recorder). The weight is piecewise-constant between updates and is drawn as steps.

updateSemantics.description: Whether the value written at an event is the weight AFTER the update that event triggered (right-continuous: it holds forward from t) or the weight that was in force BEFORE it (left-continuous: it describes the interval ending at t). Required, with no default. The two conventions differ by exactly one inter-update interval on every step edge, and Cortexel cannot tell them apart from the numbers: the wrong declaration produces an entirely plausible figure in which a potentiation appears to precede the spike that caused it. It is a property of the synapse model's send() in the exact simulator version and must be established there, not guessed.

oneOf.description: The source sampled the weight at times of its own choosing (a polled GetConnections, a clock-driven state monitor). Consecutive finite samples are joined by a straight segment only within one valid render run; missingness and membership/recording availability transitions break runs. A surviving segment is a drawing convention and not a measurement.

A caller/source-system reconstruction between supplied vertices. Revision 2 accepts only a linear interpolant because the canonical renderer draws straight segments; richer interpolants would otherwise be silently misrepresented. Its vertices are NOT observations: they never enter the observation count, and such a series may not be a member of a derived aggregate under a hold evaluation. Cortexel records and displays the reconstruction; it never performs one.

method.description: The name of the algorithm that produced the trajectory. Required: a reconstructed line that will not name its method is an unattributable curve.

interpolant.description: Revision 2 renders exactly the supplied vertices with straight segments and therefore accepts only `linear`. Holds belong to `event_updated`; spline and other reconstructions require a future geometry/authority carrier that can represent them exactly.

series.description: The synapses, in canonical identity order — one drawn time series per synapse. The array is named `series` and each synapse's weight quantity is named `values` so the registered trace validators read the actual carriers. Series order fixes table, legend and palette identity order. Exact mean and Type-7 order statistics are permutation-invariant; revision 2 reports no min/max extremizer identity or tie-break claim.

edgeId.description: Stable per-SYNAPSE identity. Two parallel synapses between one pair are two edge ids and are never merged. A source that cannot attribute a weight event to a single synapse cannot supply this.

endpoints.description: The presynaptic source and postsynaptic target. Descriptive: this figure declares no node universe and makes no degree or connectivity claim. Both ids are required together — a half-identified synapse names nothing.

synapseModel.description: The source system's synapse model — which IS the plasticity rule (`stdp_synapse`, `tsodyks2_synapse`, `static_synapse`). Required, recorded verbatim, and never used to infer a unit, a bound, or a comparability claim. Two numbers are not comparable merely because both are called `weight`.

recordedInterval.description: The span over which the source was actually observing THIS synapse. Required. A held step is drawn only inside it, and the last observed value is never extended past it: without this declaration, holding to the window edge would silently assert that no update occurred after the recorder stopped.

time.description: This synapse's observation times. Every value is finite: an observation with an unknown time is not an observation, so `null` is not accepted here — unlike a weight, where null is a legitimate missing value.

values.description: The observed weights for this synapse. Named `values` (not `weights`) so `trace.axis_dimension_compatible` reads `data.series[].values.unit` and refuses to pool weights of different dimensions on one axis. Written out rather than $ref'd to `quantitySeries` because the accepted `kind` is exactly one: a membrane potential or a firing rate on this axis would be a routing error, not a weight trace.

unit.description: A code whose dimension is legal for `synaptic_weight`: `nest:weight` (simulator-defined, never converted), a conductance, a current, a voltage, or `1`. A simulator-defined weight has no SI mapping and is never pooled with any other code.

values.description: `null` is a MISSING observation and it BREAKS THE HOLD: the interval to the next observed update is undefined and drawn as a gap. Holding the previous value across it would assert that the update did nothing.

eventKinds.description: Optional for event-updated and point-sample sources, exactly parallel to `time`: when present it has one entry per source observation and reaches the table as the source-event column. `poll` marks a value that was read rather than written. It is forbidden for interpolated_trajectory because reconstruction points are not source events; their distinct reconstruction carrier and method/interpolant/author metadata provide the relevant provenance without inventing a source-event classification.

initialWeight.description: Optional. The weight at the start of the recorded interval, before the first observed update. It closes the leading gap under `value_after_update`, appears as an explicit declared-initial-state table carrier whenever that hold is directly painted or the state is consumed by a derived aggregate, and lets the member contribute to an aggregate before its first update. It is a DECLARATION and must say where it came from. In a derived aggregate its unit code must exactly equal the member values' unit so an independently rounded conversion never enters the statistic.

origin.description: Required source metadata. A model parameter is a fact about the run; a caller assertion is a fact about the caller, and the table says which.

bounds.description: Optional reference bounds (an STDP `Wmax`, a floor at zero). When `showReferenceLines` is true they are drawn for member series that the chosen display actually shows (`individual` or `aggregate_derived_with_members`); aggregate-only derived display intentionally omits member references. Every emitted line is annotated with its origin. Cortexel NEVER clamps, rounds, or corrects an observed weight against a bound: a value observed above a hard bound stays where it was observed, because clamping it would turn the figure into evidence that the bound was respected.

boundKind.description: hard: the rule clips at the bound. soft: the rule approaches it asymptotically. The distinction changes how a flat trace at the bound should be read, and it is the caller's declaration.

uncertainty.description: Required on every edge and semantically fixed to `{"kind":"none","reason":...}` in revision 2. This series is one identified synapse from one run; no repeat universe, alignment, central estimator, or repeat-level carrier exists to substantiate a non-none uncertainty. Future repeat-aware input needs a new contract rather than an unbound basis label.

sourceRef.description: Optional content-addressed reference to the bytes this trace was extracted from. Cortexel RECORDS it and never resolves it — stable core has no filesystem or network authority — and it never substitutes for the inline observations.

membership.description: The group whose aggregate is drawn, and WHEN each synapse belonged to it. Required for any derived aggregate. Membership over time is not a refinement: a synapse created by structural plasticity at 500 ms did not exist before, and counting it from 0 ms drags the mean toward its initial weight at times when it was not there.

groupLabel.description: Display text. The accessible summary always prints the member count beside it, so a label cannot do the work of a completeness claim that no scope in this contract supports.

unit.description: The time unit of the intervals below. Declared explicitly rather than inherited: a compatible unit such as seconds is converted exactly once into the window unit, while an incompatible dimension or an unrepresentable conversion is refused instead of silently shrinking or moving the group.

members.description: A set-like selection over declared edge ids. Array permutation does not change numeric aggregation or identity order: the selected members retain the canonical order of `data.series`; duplicate membership ids are refused.

edgeId.description: Must resolve to exactly one declared edge. In derived mode the membership id set and data.series id set are equal: neither foreign members nor unused raw series are accepted.

intervals.description: The half-open [enter, exit) spans during which this synapse belonged to the group. A synapse that was created, pruned, and re-created has several.

oneOf.description: Pre-aggregated mode: the caller supplies an aggregate it computed elsewhere. Cortexel never saw the members, so it cannot re-derive the value — it checks what it can (lengths, counts, bounds, the denominator rules) and discloses that the individual synapses were not supplied and cannot be shown.

synapseModel.description: The one synapse model whose weights were aggregated. Revision 2 has only this scalar carrier, so a caller-declared aggregate spanning several models is not representable and must be split rather than encoded as a comma-joined pseudo-model.

method.description: The statistic that was computed. `sum` is absent by design: over a membership that changes size it conflates a change in the number of synapses with a change in their weights, and no registered disclosure can carry that conflation.

intervalMethod.description: How the caller obtained each member's value at each evaluation time. Required: an aggregate of event-updated weights is meaningless until it says what it assumed about the times between the updates. `declared_by_source` is an honest admission that the upstream system's rule is not known in this detail, and it is disclosed as such.

observation.description: How the AGGREGATE SERIES itself is to be read and drawn. The mean of right-continuous step functions is itself a right-continuous step function, so a hold-evaluated mean of event-updated weights is `event_updated` and is drawn as steps — not as a smooth line through its evaluation points.

interpolant.description: Revision 2's canonical geometry is a straight segment between supplied aggregate vertices, so only `linear` is accepted.

time.description: The evaluation times of the aggregate. Finite; no nulls.

values.description: The aggregate at each evaluation time. `null` where no member contributed — never 0.

memberCounts.description: How many synapses belonged to the group at each evaluation time. This is what makes a CHANGING membership visible: a mean that rises while this count falls is a different fact from a mean that rises while it holds.

contributingCounts.description: How many of those members actually had a value at that time. THIS is the denominator of a mean — never the member count, never the number of synapses in the network. It must be <= the member count, it must be positive wherever a value is present, and where it is 0 the value must be null.

uncertainty.description: Required. The descriptive spread across the contributing member synapses at each evaluation time, or an explicit `none`. A non-none carrier must use basis `ensemble_members` and provide sampleCount; distinct synapses are not presumed to be statistical replicates. Empirical quantile/range counts equal positive contributingCounts, and quantile_interval method is exactly `empirical_type_7_linear`; sample standard deviation is allowed only with a mean, equals contributingCounts only at two or more contributors, and is null below that threshold. Range/quantile bounds must be mathematically compatible with the declared aggregate method and collapse to the sole aggregate value at one contributor. Standard error, confidence intervals, and credible intervals are refused because this request carries no inferential sampling or posterior design.

display.description: individual: one drawn series per synapse, with a legend entry each; refused above 8 edges — the palette's `categoricalSeries.maxStableSeries` — rather than minting a style outside the colour-vision-deficiency-safe set. aggregate_derived: Cortexel computes the aggregate from the member observations. aggregate_derived_with_members: the same, with the members drawn behind it WITHOUT individual colour or legend identity, so the spread is visible while no per-synapse colour claim is made. aggregate_declared: the caller's pre-aggregated series, which Cortexel cannot re-derive. The first three require `data.mode: edges`; the last requires `data.mode: preaggregated`.

weightComparability.description: Why these weights may share one value axis at all. Required, because `nest:weight` is one code covering values whose physical meaning differs by synapse and neuron model: the same number 10.0 may be a 10 pA postsynaptic current under one model and a 10 nS conductance under another.

oneOf.description: Every drawn weight comes from the same synapse model. If two or more distinct `synapseModel` values are present, this fails with SCIENCE_WEIGHT_GROUP_INCOMPATIBLE.

The caller declares that the listed models produce weights that mean the same physical thing. The set of distinct models present must equal this list exactly. Cortexel verifies that the claim was made and that it matches the data; it cannot verify that it is TRUE, and the declared set is surfaced in the summary and the table, attributed to the caller.

aggregate.description: How Cortexel is to DERIVE the aggregate from the declared members. Required for `aggregate_derived` and `aggregate_derived_with_members`; forbidden for `individual` and for `aggregate_declared`, where there is nothing to derive.

method.description: mean divides by the CONTRIBUTING count, never the member count. median is the Type-7 0.5 quantile. min/max return the exact extreme value; revision 2 reports no extremizer identity or tie set.

evaluation.description: The interval method: how a member's value at an evaluation time is obtained. There is no interpolating mode and there never will be — an interpolated weight between two updates is a value that never existed.

oneOf.description: Evaluate at the exact discontinuity closure: window start, every accepted member observation, membership start/stop, effective recorded start/stop, and an inclusive window stop. Holding is legal only when `data.observation.kind` is `event_updated`; including every state boundary prevents a value or denominator from being held across a change that is absent from the table.

Evaluate at an explicit grid, holding each member's last observed value. Legal only for `event_updated`, and the grid MUST contain the complete discontinuity closure used by the union mode. Extra read times are allowed; omitting an observation, membership, recording or initial-state boundary is refused because the rendered aggregate is a complete step carrier, not a sparse point sample.

Aggregate per sample index over an identical accepted time vector. Legal only for `point_sample`. Repeated samples must first be collapsed by the declared within-synapse aggregate method; `keep_replicates` is refused because no cross-member replicate identity licenses pairing by ordinal. Nothing is held or interpolated.

dispersion.description: The spread across the CONTRIBUTING member synapses at each evaluation time, computed by Cortexel from the members it was given. It is a library-generated fact, which is why it is requested by name here rather than supplied as values.

oneOf.description: One sample standard deviation across the contributing members, descriptive only and permitted only around a mean. It is NOT an interval, standard error, or population claim and is never relabelled as one.
Empirical quantiles of the contributing members. It carries no coverage probability and is never drawn or labelled as a confidence interval.
The observed minimum and maximum across the contributing members. No coverage probability, and never drawn as a confidence interval.

duplicateTimePolicy.description: What to do when one synapse has two carriers at one timestamp. Enforced semantically when duplicates actually exist. Revision 2 refuses actual `keep_replicates` for all weight kinds: event rows have no side/order identity, repeated point samples would be connected as an invented within-time trajectory, and two reconstruction y values at one x are not a function. Point samples and reconstruction vertices may instead be collapsed by one named method. Collapsing a series with non-none per-row uncertainty is refused because no propagation model is declared. The keep branch remains structurally parseable so this scientific refusal is explicit rather than downgraded to a generic schema error.

oneOf.description: Applied within one edge at one timestamp over non-null repeated point samples or linear reconstruction vertices; an even-count median is the Type-7 0.5 quantile. The source-row multiplicity reaches the table. Event-updated duplicates may not aggregate, and actual duplicate keep_replicates is refused for every weight kind.

showObservationMarkers.description: Draw optional markers at every finite, marker-eligible displayed source observation, reconstruction vertex, and aggregate-evaluation carrier. Missing, table-only, or off-membership carriers remain unmarked. Presentation only: it changes no value or authority class. Reconstruction markers remain labelled reconstruction nodes; derived state-boundary evaluations remain derived carriers. One exception is contract-owned rather than optional: an included zero-duration event update at a closed terminal edge always receives an authority-bound terminal marker so it cannot be omitted from the RenderPlan.

showReferenceLines.description: Draw declared initial weights and bounds for series present in the selected display (`individual`, `aggregate_derived_with_members`, or a caller-declared aggregate) as reference lines annotated with their declared origin. An aggregate-only derived display omits raw-member reference lines. Declarations are never used to clamp or correct an observed value.

parameters.note: This generic pointer evaluator remains a compatibility backstop for the three living edge examples and the pre-aggregated carrier. A group is compared only within itself: one synapse's time array is checked against that SAME synapse's weight array and never against another synapse's, because two synapses legitimately carry different numbers of updates. The dedicated `weight_trace.observation_kind_declared` evaluator dynamically checks every declared series and all pre-aggregated arrays, so correctness does not depend on this registry's lack of an index wildcard.

accessibility.summaryTemplate: Synaptic weight trace. Cardinality: {synapseCardinalityStatement} Model {synapseModels}, weight in {weightUnit} ({weightDimensionStatement}), over {windowStart} to {windowStop} {timeUnit}. Observation semantics: {observationStatement}. {duplicateTimeStatement} Source evidence: {sourceReadingCount} raw readings in {retainedSourceRowCount} retained source rows; {missingCount} raw readings are missing and {excludedCount} are outside the window. Reconstruction evidence: {reconstructionPointCount} raw vertices in {retainedReconstructionRowCount} retained reconstruction rows; {missingReconstructionPointCount} retained vertices are missing and {excludedReconstructionPointCount} are outside the window. {carrierStatement} Display: {displayMode}. {aggregateStatement} {membershipStatement} {referenceStatement} Scope: {scopeStatement}. {uncertaintyStatement} {compactionStatement}

tableColumns.description: The edge id, or the group id on an aggregate row. Two parallel synapses between one pair are two ids and are never merged.
Presynaptic node. Descriptive: this figure declares no node universe and makes no connectivity claim. Empty on an aggregate row.
Postsynaptic node. Empty on an aggregate row.

Every row carries canonical structured text containing its exact actual model set, the complete caller-declared model set, and the `weightComparability` mode. A raw row therefore remains auditable after detachment from its request, while a derived aggregate preserves the ordered model set; physical dimension compatibility alone is never presented as a model-level comparability proof.
In the window's declared unit, after any recorded conversion.
The observed weight, or the aggregate on an aggregate row. Empty when the observation is missing. A missing observation is not zero.
A `nest:weight` is simulator-defined: no SI mapping, never converted, never compared across systems.

event_updated (held, emitted as steps), point_sample (consecutive finite samples joined by straight segments only within one valid render run), or interpolated_trajectory (supplied linear reconstruction vertices, not observations). Missingness and membership/recording availability transitions break applicable runs.
value_after_update: this value holds forward from its time. value_before_update: it describes the interval ending at its time. The two differ by one inter-update interval on every step edge.

Canonical structured {from,until,unit} only when this carrier owns an emitted positive-duration event hold in the exact frozen RenderPlan runs. `from` and `until` are the endpoints of that horizontal RenderPlan span, not a separate claim about mathematical endpoint closure or pixel visibility. The span is clipped by the analysis window, recorded interval and any membership lifetime. Null for table-only carriers, missing values, point/reconstruction vertices and closed-stop singleton points.

Zero-based ordinal of the exact explicit primary trace run owned by this carrier within the frozen RenderPlan. A run may be a line/path carrier sequence or a contract-mandated singleton marker. Within line/path geometry, equal ordinals identify the same explicit RenderPlan run and different ordinals require distinct RenderPlan subpaths. Null means the carrier owns no primary run. Optional observation/reconstruction markers do not create a run, while a mandatory closed-stop singleton is itself an explicit singleton run with an ordinal but no line segment or paintedInterval. The ordinal is bound into primary line/path provenance where such a subpath exists. This is a RenderPlan topology check, not assurance of serialized SVG paths, clipping, or pixel visibility.
What produced the observation: a presynaptic spike, a structural update, a poll of the stored value, or a parameter write.
true when the weight was null in the source. A missing update breaks the hold; the following interval is undefined, not unchanged.

For a retained source-observation or reconstruction row, how many raw caller rows produced this carrier. Greater than one identifies a named duplicate-time aggregate. Null on derived evaluations, initial states, and context witnesses.
Aggregate rows: how many synapses belonged to the group at this time. A mean that rises while this falls is a different fact from a mean that rises while it holds.
Aggregate rows: how many members actually had a value at this time. THIS is the denominator of the mean. Where it is 0 the aggregate is empty, never zero.
The variant, level and basis. A dispersion across synapses is never relabelled as a confidence interval.

The declared value and its origin (a model parameter or a caller assertion). It may seed an explicitly attributed leading hold or derived aggregate, and is drawn as an additional reference line only when requested. It never clamps or corrects an observed value.

The declared lower/upper bound, whether it is hard or soft, and its origin. A weight observed beyond a hard bound is reported here and, when that member series is displayed, remains at its observed value; aggregate-only display may hide member geometry but retains the exact row.

One of source_observation, source_state_witness, caller_reconstruction_point, derived_aggregate_evaluation, declared_aggregate_point, or declared_initial_state. This discriminator prevents a source row, context state witness, reconstruction vertex, initial state, and aggregate evaluation at one time from being conflated; a context witness may itself be directly painted.
Zero-based ordinal within this series and carrier kind after exact preparation. Together with series, time, and carrierKind it gives every returned carrier a unique row identity.

Canonical structured metadata owned by the carrier kind. Reconstruction rows bind method/interpolant/author; every duplicate-collapsed source/reconstruction carrier binds the named aggregate policy and method; context witnesses bind carry-in/look-ahead, directly-painted and derived-consultation status; initial-state rows bind declaration time separately from direct paint and derived consumption. Null where no extra carrier metadata is required.

Canonical scalar/array lineage into caller rows before within-edge stable sorting and named duplicate collapse. Present for retained source observations, reconstruction vertices, and source-state witnesses; null for derived evaluations and declared initial states.

A bounded canonical projection of the scope this synapse was observed under. `global_merged` records complete-rank coverage and world size without repeating `mergedRanks`; the exact raw scope remains once in the canonical request and is covered by the live in-process request digest. Artifact 1.0 binds this table's shape only, not table-cell bytes, and supplies no detached verification or persisted assurance receipt. A rank-local snapshot never licenses a claim about another rank's synapses.

externalOracle.name: NEST stdp_synapse + weight_recorder microfixture (pinned), cross-checked by hand against the analytic STDP update
externalOracle.notes: There is no library-independent oracle for most of this contract. The hold semantics, the membership-aware denominators, and the missing-update break are conventions of THIS contract and have no equivalent in Elephant, Neo, or NWB, so they rest entirely on the hand vectors. The one thing an external oracle can settle is the question this figure is most easily wrong about: whether the value a NEST weight recorder writes at a presynaptic spike is the weight BEFORE or AFTER the update that spike triggered. That is a property of stdp_synapse::send() in the pinned version, it must be established by executing a two-spike microfixture whose expected weights were computed by hand from the STDP kernel, and until it is, the adapter must not assume a convention — it must require the caller to declare one. The pinned reference environment has NOT been executed in this environment: the status is not_run, and it is not to be reported as anything else.

adapters.notes: The device records one event per transmitted spike per plastic synapse, carrying the sender, the target, the time (ms) and the weight (`nest:weight`). Two limits make this PARTIAL rather than supported. First, the record carries no stable per-synapse identity, so two parallel synapses (a multapse) between the same pair cannot be told apart: the adapter must refuse to mint an edge id that would merge or split them, and it proceeds only when the caller declares that at most one synapse exists per (source, target) pair. Second, whether the written weight precedes or follows the update triggered by that spike is a property of the synapse model's send() in the exact NEST version; the adapter NEVER assumes it and requires `observation.updateSemantics` to be declared. It also requires the recorded interval (the device's start/stop), the synapse model, and the scope, none of which the event stream contains. Under MPI the device is rank-local: a rank holds the synapses whose TARGET it owns, so the adapter emits `mpi_target_rank_local` and never a global claim.

Repeatedly calling GetConnections(...).get('weight') at declared times yields `point_sample` observations of the STORED weight. Two facts must travel with them. NEST's STDP synapses are updated lazily, only at a presynaptic spike, so a poll returns the last APPLIED weight and not one that includes plasticity pending from postsynaptic activity since. And a poll sees only the moments it looked: an update and a counter-update that both fall between two polls cancel and leave no trace, so a polled trace can never establish that a synapse was quiet.

Brian records synaptic variables on a regular clock. For an event-driven (on_pre / on_post) rule the underlying variable is piecewise-constant between events while the monitor samples it on the clock, so the samples are point samples OF a step function: joining them with straight segments invents ramps that the model never had, and the mapping must therefore either declare `point_sample` honestly or reconstruct the update events. Not certified until a version matrix and fixtures exist.

Neither models a synaptic weight. Forcing a weight trace into a Neo AnalogSignal or an NWB TimeSeries would lose the synapse identity, the update semantics, and the simulator-defined unit, and would present a lazily-updated step function as a regularly sampled continuous signal. A weight trace is not an analog trace: `neuro.analog_trace` closes its quantity-kind enum against `synaptic_weight` for exactly this reason.

An NCP adapter must consume an already-authenticated, already-admitted observation from an immutable pinned NCP release. NCP's declarations are carried, never upgraded: `is_simulation_output=true` becomes `source.kind: simulation`, and an absent calibrated-posterior claim stays absent.

migration.notes: The legacy id drew plasticity dynamics as a line through whatever it was handed: no observation semantics, no hold convention, no recorded interval, no synapse model, and, for group curves, no denominator and no membership over time. An event-updated weight became a ramp between updates, the last value ran to the plot edge, weights of different physical meaning shared one axis, and a synapse created halfway through the run counted from the start. None of it is recoverable, so migration REQUIRES an observation kind, an update semantics for event-updated data, a weight unit, a synapse model, a recorded interval, a scope, and a membership with intervals for a group curve. Otherwise it BLOCKS with MIGRATION_INFORMATION_MISSING rather than defaulting to a plausible, publishable, wrong figure.

changePolicy: Changing what a step asserts — the hold direction implied by `value_after_update` / `value_before_update` — is MAJOR. It moves every step edge by one inter-update interval in every stored artifact while leaving the figure structurally identical.
Changing null semantics (a missing update breaks the hold and is never held across, never zero) is MAJOR.
Changing the aggregate's denominator from the contributing count to the member count, or to any other basis, is MAJOR.
Changing the rule that a zero contributing count yields a null aggregate rather than 0.0 is MAJOR.

Changing canonical series identity order is MAJOR because it changes table, legend, palette and artifact identity order. Exact mean, Type-7 quantiles and scalar min/max values are permutation-invariant; revision 2 carries no extremizer tie-break claim.
Changing the median's even-count rule is MAJOR.
Changing the refusal to clamp an observed weight against a declared bound is MAJOR.
Changing the requirement for a per-edge recorded interval, or extending a held value past it, is MAJOR.
Changing the half-open convention of the window or of a membership interval is MAJOR.
Adding an interpolating evaluation mode to a derived aggregate would be MAJOR, and it is refused outright today.
Adding `sum` as an aggregate method is MINOR, but only once a disclosure exists that carries the conflation between a change in membership size and a change in weight.

Adding an uncertainty variant to uncertaintySupport is MINOR. Adding a non-linear interpolant to `interpolated_trajectory` is MAJOR until the geometry, clipping, table, and authority contracts can carry that reconstruction exactly; Cortexel records reconstructions and never computes one.
knownLimitations: Cortexel cannot verify the update convention. It records the caller's `updateSemantics` and draws it. A wrong declaration produces a fully plausible figure whose every step edge is displaced by one inter-update interval, and no validator can detect it from the numbers.

NEST's weight recorder exposes no per-synapse identity, so parallel synapses between one pair cannot be separated from recorder output alone. This is a limit of the source, not of the contract, and the adapter refuses rather than inventing an edge id.

Cross-dimension pooling on the value axis IS enforced: `trace.axis_dimension_compatible` reads `data.series[].values.unit` and refuses two synapses of different dimensions (for example `nest:weight` and `nS`) on one axis, because this figure declares no small-multiples layout.

Duplicate carrier times are checked across every edge by the generic policy validator and the weight-specific coherence boundary. Revision 2 refuses every actual `keep_replicates` duplicate: event rows lack same-time side/event identity, repeated point samples would become an invented vertical trajectory, and two reconstruction values at one time are not a function. Point-sample and linear-reconstruction duplicates may instead use a named aggregate because raw edge uncertainty is fixed to none; shared-grid pairing of unresolved replicates remains refused because cross-member replicate identity is absent.

Cortexel-derived hold aggregates support only `value_after_update` and half-open recorded intervals in revision 2. Side-qualified denominator transitions and a terminal value-before carrier are not present in Artifact 1.0. Individual and caller-declared preaggregated traces still honor both update conventions and their registered recorded/window closure directly.

Weight comparability across synapse models is declared in `parameters.weightComparability`, checked as an exact duplicate-free set against every raw series or the declared pre-aggregate model, and shown in the table. This establishes only that the caller made a complete claim matching the models present; Cortexel still cannot establish that distinct models' weights are physically comparable.

Every identified raw edge is limited to `uncertainty:none`: one synapse from one run supplies no aligned repeat universe or repeat-level evidence for a per-time SD, SE, interval, or reconstructed band. Caller-declared preaggregates and Cortexel-derived aggregates may name descriptive across-synapse SD (around a mean), empirical quantiles, or observed range with `basis: ensemble_members`; that states only that the values are concurrent members of the exact declared ensemble. Standard error and confidence intervals are unavailable because no sampling design, estimand, exchangeability claim, repeat universe, or coverage procedure exists.

There is no error code for a weight observed beyond a declared HARD bound. Cortexel never clamps and reports the violation in the table; when that member series and its requested reference are displayed, the observed value and declared bound retain their values. Aggregate-only display may omit raw-member geometry and references, but the exact row remains in the complete table. The value is the measurement; the bound is the caller's claim.
`sum` is not offered. Over a membership that changes size it conflates a change in the number of synapses with a change in their weights, and no registered disclosure can carry that.

Revision 2 executes no line-envelope compaction: accepted step traces are drawn in full and an over-budget request is refused. A future line-envelope policy would have to retain every bucket extremum, bind a complete exact table, and disclose that drawn marks are not recorded updates before this skill could advertise it.

Cortexel can verify that a weight's unit dimension matches its kind. It cannot verify that two models' weights are truly comparable, that a `nest:weight` means what the caller believes, or that a declared bound is the model's.

series.label: created by structural plasticity at 500 ms

membership.groupLabel: Plastic E to E synapses (3 declared members)

source.declaredNote: Synapse e3 was created at 500 ms; it is not a member before that.

declaredLimitations: The individual synapses were not exported and cannot be shown.

invalid.why: An event-updated source with no `updateSemantics`. The weight a NEST weight recorder writes at a presynaptic spike is either the weight AFTER the update the spike triggered or the weight in force before it, and the numbers cannot tell you which. The two readings put a potentiation on opposite sides of an inter-update interval, so Cortexel refuses to guess: the schema requires the declaration and this request omits it.

Five update times, four weights, in one synapse. `series.equal_length` compares this synapse's `time.values` against its own `values.values`; a positional pairing would attach every weight to the previous update, drawing the potentiation recorded at 130 ms as though it happened at 60 ms. Cortexel never pairs values with times by best effort.

One event-updated synapse carries two source rows at t = 60 ms. Revision 2 refuses every actual event-time duplicate: request-wide before/after semantics cannot identify the side or sequential event represented by either same-time row, and array order is not scientific event-order authority. Split the events with attributable times/identity or refuse the figure.

A simulator-defined `nest:weight` synapse and a conductance weight in `nS` are placed on one value axis. `trace.axis_dimension_compatible` reads each synapse's `values.unit`: `nest:weight` has dimension simulator_defined and `nS` conductance, so drawing them at the same height is a coincidence of numerals with no physical content. There is no conversion to fall back on; the two must be separate figures.

`nS` is canonical; `nanosiemens` is only an alias. A stable request must use the canonical code and is rejected with a repair rather than silently converted, because a silent conversion changes a number the caller never sees — and between nS and S that number differs by a factor of 1,000,000,000.

`clampToBounds` is not a parameter of this figure and never will be. The declared `Wmax` here is 2.0 and the synapse was observed at 2.4: clamping would draw it at 2.0 and turn the figure into evidence that the bound was respected, hiding a 20% overshoot. The closed parameters schema makes the unknown property fail rather than be silently ignored.

The request asserts that Cortexel validated it. A validation result is a library-generated fact: a caller declares what its data IS, never what Cortexel concluded about it. The field is rejected on the RAW request, before any schema or default, so a stored artifact can never carry a passing verdict that nobody computed.

The scope claims a global merge of a 3-rank run but lists only ranks 0 and 1. A rank-local weight recorder saw only the synapses whose target its rank owns, so rank 2's synapses are simply absent. `topology.scope_supports_claim` refuses to upgrade a partial rank set to a global claim: the missing rank must be merged, or the output kept labelled as the partial evidence it is.
